comments:
This paper addresses cross-lingual transfer for paradigm completion (morphological inflection generation) by training a single encoder–decoder RNN with attention on mixed-language data and an explicit language token. The problem is well-motivated (low-resource morphology), the formulation and model are clear, and the work presents a thorough experimental evaluation across 21 language-pair settings from multiple families (Romance, Slavic, Uralic) plus an unrelated Arabic baseline. Key strengths: (i) solid formalization of cross-lingual paradigm completion as multi-task learning; (ii) use of character-level input with UNIMORPH subtags and a simple language tag to enable multilingual training; (iii) systematic experiments including learning curves, one-shot/zero-shot settings, and a useful ciphering ablation to separate true linguistic transfer from mere regularization effects; (iv) clear evidence that transfer helps most for related languages and can yield large absolute gains in low-resource regimes.

Weaknesses and suggestions: (1) Baselines are limited — the paper compares to monolingual training and an unrelated-language baseline but lacks comparison to other transfer approaches (e.g., alignment-based string transduction, non-neural methods, or simple shared-subtag models). Adding such baselines would strengthen claims. (2) Some implementation choices (no conventional regularizers, identity initialization) are uncommon; further justification or sensitivity analysis would help. (3) While several error examples are discussed, a more systematic error analysis (types of failures, role of lemma overlap, effect of diacritics/accents) would be informative. (4) Reproducibility: the paper gives many hyperparameters and cites UNIMORPH, but public release of code/data splits would improve impact.

Overall, this is a solid, well-executed contribution to low-resource morphological generation with valuable empirical insights. Recommended for acceptance after addressing the comparative-baseline and analysis concerns where possible.
